text: Research Article

An Examination of Fluoxetine for the Treatment of Selective
Mutism Using a Nonconcurrent Multiple-Baseline
Single-Case Design Across 5 Cases

This study examined the utility of ﬂuoxetine
in the treatment of 5 children, aged 5 to
14 years, diagnosed with selective mutism
who also demonstrated symptoms of social
anxiety. A nonconcurrent, randomized, multi-
ple-baseline, single-case design with a single-
blind placebo-controlled procedure was used.
Parents and the study psychiatrist completed
multiple
methods
of
assessment
including
Direct Behavior Ratings and questionnaires.
Treatment outcomes were evaluated by cal-
culating effect sizes for each participant as an
individual and for the participants as a group.
Information regarding adverse effects with an
emphasis
on
behavioral
disinhibition
and
ratings
of
parental
acceptance
of
the
intervention
was gathered. All 5 children
experienced improvement in social anxiety,
responsive speech, and spontaneous speech
with medium to large effect sizes; however,
children still met criteria for selective mutism
at the end of the study. Adverse events were
minimal, with only 2 children experiencing
brief occurrences of minor behavioral dis-
inhibition.
Parents
found
the
treatment
highly acceptable.
(Journal of Psychiatric Practice 2018;24;2–14)

KEY WORDS: selective mutism, social anxiety,
psychopharmacology, children, adolescents

Selective mutism, which is typically diagnosed by
age 5 years, is a rare anxiety disorder affecting
fewer than 1% of children.1 The disorder is charac-
terized by the withholding of verbal communication
in certain situations (eg, school, grocery store) while
displaying appropriate speech in settings in which
the child is comfortable.1 The disorder frequently
co-occurs with social anxiety.2,3 Selective mutism
results in impaired social4 and academic5 function-
ing, and affected children are more likely to expe-
rience a psychiatric disorder as an adult.6 When
psychosocial
approaches
such
as
systematic

desensitization7 fail, selective serotonin reuptake
inhibitors (SSRIs), which have demonstrated efﬁ-
cacy for anxiety,8 are a logical treatment option. In
practice settings, SSRIs are frequently used to treat
selective mutism.9 Therefore, studies that system-
atically analyze the outcomes of this approach are
needed to provide physicians and parents with the
information necessary to conduct an analysis of
potential risks and beneﬁts. Results of the research
that does exist is encouraging,10 with the most
evidence concerning the off label use of ﬂuoxetine.9

However, the available studies possess ﬂaws.
Two small studies and several case studies have
examined the efﬁcacy of ﬂuoxetine for selective
mutism. Black and Uhde11 used a quasi-experimental
group design with a repeated measure analysis of
variance to examine the difference in speaking
behaviors and anxiety between a placebo/control
group (n = 9) and an experimental/ﬂuoxetine group
(n = 6; 12 to 27 mg/d for 12 wk). Children receiving
ﬂuoxetine displayed greater improvement in mutism
symptoms. No side effects were observed. Despite
improvements in symptomology, the conclusions were
limited by the sample size (n = 15) used for the stat-
istical analysis (ie, analysis of variance) and by dif-
ferences in baseline scores between groups. In an open
label trial, Dummit et al12 examined the use of
ﬂuoxetine in 21 participants aged 5 to 14 years. Doses

2

Journal of Psychiatric Practice Vol. 24, No. 1
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

of ﬂuoxetine ranged from 5 up to 60 mg/d based on
response. Improvements in social anxiety and speak-
ing were noted. Minor side effects (eg, headache,
insomnia) were reported by 43% of participants. Two
participants discontinued treatment due to symptoms
of
behavioral
disinhibition
(eg,
hyperactivity).
Although these ﬁndings showed promise, the design
did not include a control group, and t tests were used
to assess improvement, despite the fact that the
sample size was small for this statistical approach.
Seven case studies reporting on the use of ﬂuoxetine to
treat selective mutism have been published, and all
reported improvements such as increased speech at
school. For example, in a case reported by Wright
et al,13 a 5-year-old girl taking ﬂuoxetine 4 to 8 mg/d
began speaking within 20 days in several settings.
Despite
improvements
in
symptoms
across
case
studies, the absence of systematic analysis of out-
comes is a substantial limitation.
The small sample sizes in the studies by Black
and Uhde11 and Dummit et al12 and in the several
available case studies are limiting; however, given
the rarity of selective mutism, recruitment for a
large clinical trial is impractical. Therefore, other
methodologically sound approaches that provide
meaningful results despite a small sample size
need to be applied. Single-case designs provide
experimental control and internal validity with a
small sample similar to the control provided in
randomized control-group experimental designs.14

In
addition,
single-case
design
methodology
addresses some of the weaknesses associated with
randomized
control
trials and traditional
sig-
niﬁcance testing. For example, Carver15 noted that
results
from
randomized
control
trials
were
frequently reported as meaningful because of a
P-value <0.05. However, even though the results
were statistically signiﬁcant, when examined more
closely, the intervention did not have a clinically
important impact on the outcome variable for
individual participants. Single-case design meth-
odologies achieve 3 goals: (1) identify a meaningful
change in a dependent variable in a small sample;
(2) determine if the change in the dependent
variable was a direct result of the application
of an independent variable; and (3) document
an
experimental
control.16
Previous
research
involving
children
diagnosed
with
selective
mutism has noted the utility of single-case design
methodologies.9

In addition to limitations related to sample size,
researchers have not documented the type of speech
children engage in during treatment. Delineating
between spontaneous speech (ie, speaking for com-
munication)
and
responsive
speech
(ie,
brieﬂy
responding to questions) is essential, as spontaneous
speech is the desired goal.17 Moreover, previous SSRI
studies have not investigated the mechanism of action
leading to improvements. Conventionally, SSRIs are
expected to take 4 to 6 weeks to improve symptomol-
ogy. However, earlier research examining the use of
SSRIs for the treatment of selective mutism found
rapid improvements in speaking.9 To explain this
phenomenon, some have suggested that behavioral
disinhibition, which is deﬁned as increased motor
activity, impulsiveness, insomnia, and/or excitement18

may be mimicking improvement.19 Therefore, the
examination of the onset of behavioral disinhibition
concurrently with improvements in symptomology is
necessary.9 Finally, because physicians must weigh
the risks and beneﬁts of prescribing a psycho-
pharmacological medication for any child or adoles-
cent, parent acceptability ratings of ﬂuoxetine for the
treatment of selective mutism are also essential. Two
previous studies that examined parent acceptability
ratings for the use of sertraline to treat selective
mutism symptoms found high parent acceptability
ratings.19,20

This study used a multiple-baseline, single-case
design intended to identify a treatment effect in a small
sample to examine the utility of the off label use of
ﬂuoxetine for the treatment of selective mutism. Given
the ﬁndings from previous work, it was hypothesized
that ﬂuoxetine treatment would result in an increase in
social engagement, responsive speech, and spontaneous
speech. Furthermore, the researchers expected behav-
ioral disinhibition would not solely coincide with
improvements in selective mutism symptomology, side
effects would be minimal, and parents would ﬁnd the
treatment highly acceptable.
METHODS
Participants

Journal of Psychiatric Practice Vol. 24, No. 1
January 2018
3
FLUOXETINE TREATMENT FOR SELECTIVE MUTISM
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

had experienced a negative reaction to a psycho-
pharmacological medication; or had an illness that
could be exacerbated by treatment. Invitations to
participate were extended to those who met criteria
for selective mutism using the ﬁfth edition of the
Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Dis-
orders (DSM-5)1 and who had previously completed
at least 12 weeks of an evidence-based psychosocial
treatment. Finally, children had to demonstrate
symptoms of social anxiety as indicated by a T score
> 65 (moderately atypical) on the second edition of
the Multidimensional Anxiety Scale for Children-2
(MASC-2): Social Anxiety Scale (SAS)/Performance
Fear Scale.21 T-scores on the MASC-2 are only pro-
vided for children who are 8 years old or older.
Therefore, participants younger than 8 years old
were evaluated based on the T-scores for 8-year-old
children, given there are no normed assessments
that examine anxiety symptoms in younger children.
Procedures

The Michigan State University-Biomedical Institu-
tional Review Board (MSU-BIRB) approved the
procedures.
Bi-weekly
medication
management
appointments at the MSU psychiatry clinic and the
ﬂuoxetine medication were provided without charge.
Because of the young age of 1 participant (ie, 5 y old),
the project psychiatrist was required by the MSU-
BIRB to write a treatment summary following every
medication management meeting for this child,
which was reviewed by a safety committee consisting
of a pediatrician and a child and adolescent psy-
chiatrist who were not afﬁliated with the project in
any other way. The members individually reviewed

the
information
and
approved
all
treatment
decisions.
Independent Variables

Five treatment schedules were determined a priori
that varied by the length of time children received the
placebo before receiving ﬂuoxetine. Participants were
randomly assigned to 1 of these treatment schedules
in
accordance
with
the
multiple-baseline,
single
case design procedure. The psychiatrist was aware of
the treatment schedules and prescribed the medication
accordingly. Children with selective mutism have been
found to demonstrate little response to placebo.19,20

However, to account for the possibility of a placebo
effect,
a
1-week
no-medication
component
was
included in the baseline phase, with all participants
beginning to take the placebo at week 2. Depending on
the
randomized
treatment
schedule,
participants
began taking 10 mg/d of ﬂuoxetine at week 4 (ie, child
1) through week 8 (ie, child 5). After 2 weeks at 10 mg/
d, the psychiatrist increased to a therapeutic dose of
20 mg/d for all children (Table 1). The elixirs for the 10
and 20 mg/d doses were identical in ﬂavor, color, con-
sistency, and quantity (ie, mL) to the placebo elixir.
Dependent Variables

Social Anxiety and Frequency of Speech
Direct Behavior Rating (DBR)22 was the primary
outcome measure of social anxiety symptomology
and speaking behaviors. Parents received structured
training on how to conduct DBRs that included
instruction on common rater errors (eg, halo effect),
modeling, and practice using videotaped scenarios.

FLUOXETINE TREATMENT FOR SELECTIVE MUTISM
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

Parents were asked to observe their child in a social
interaction with an unfamiliar adult 3 times per
week. After each observation, parents rated the
amount of social engagement displayed on a 10-point
scale ranging from 0 (never) to 10 (always). For
ratings
of
social
engagement,
parents
were
instructed not to base the ratings on speaking
behaviors, as the purpose of this rating was to
determine frequency and ease of social engagement.
Parents also provided ratings for spontaneous and
responsive speech for their child’s interactions with
unfamiliar adults 3 times per week on the same
scale. Given the need for frequent ratings, System-
atic Direct Observations (SDOs) requiring parents to
document individual behaviors for a predetermined
amount of time were not practical. However, SDOs
and DBRs are signiﬁcantly correlated (r ≥0.81,
P < 0.01), with regression analysis showing that
DBRs account for 76% of the variance of SDOs.23

An adapted Clinical Global Impressions scale
(CGI)19,24 was used as a supplementary measure of
anxiety, shyness, and mutism symptoms. Ratings
were provided by parents twice per week and by the
study
psychiatrist
at
medication
management
appointments. Parents and psychiatrists provided
severity ratings on 5-point Likert scales ranging
from 1 (absent) to 5 (severe) and global change
ratings on 7-point Likert scales ranging from 1
(much improved) to 7 (much worse) across home,
school, and community settings. The CGI technique
has demonstrated concurrent validity with other
assessment procedures.25

The MASC-2: SAS,21 which is a 9-item parent-
report measure for children between the ages of 8
and 19 years, was used as a supplementary measure
of social anxiety. Parents completed the ratings
twice per week. The MASC-2: SAS has excellent
test-retest reliability (r = 0.90, P < 0.001), and sig-
niﬁcantly correlates with Conner’s Comprehensive
Behavior Rating Scales-Parent (r = 0.55, P < 0.01).26

The Selective Mutism Questionnaire (SMQ)27 is a
17-item parent-report assessment used to deter-
mine the severity of mutism symptoms. The SMQ
was completed by parents twice per week as a
supplementary measure of speaking behaviors. The
SMQ has an internal consistency coefﬁcient of
0.9727 and demonstrates concurrent validity with
the Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule for Chil-
dren for DSM-IV-Parent Version (ADIS/CP) with a
correlation coefﬁcient of 0.48.28,29

Adverse Events and Behavioral Disinhibition
A 12-item adapted version of the Side Effects
Form for Children and Adolescents (SEFCA)30 was
completed at each medication management meeting
by the psychiatrist. The adapted SEFCA, which
included adverse events associated with SSRIs that
occur in 5% or more of children and adolescents,31

such as drowsiness, difﬁculty falling asleep, and
irritability,
was
used
to
assess
the
frequency
and severity of side effects. An item capturing
behavioral
disinhibition
was
also
included
to
monitor this potentially confounding behavioral
response.
The Parent-Young Mania Rating Scale (P-YMRS)32

was used to assess the occurrence of behavioral dis-
inhibition and was completed twice per week by
parents. The measure consists of 11 multiple-choice
questions and was designed to examine manic
symptoms in children 5 to 17 years of age. The
P-YMRS has an internal consistency of 0.75, and has
adequate discriminative validity between bipolar
disorder and other mental health diagnoses. As the
purpose of the P-YMRS in this study was to identify
behavioral disinhibition associated with the onset of
ﬂuoxetine treatment, this measure was not scored
conventionally. Instead, item scores of “3” or higher
were considered to indicate elevated symptoms of
behavioral disinhibition on all items except for an
item concerning speech behaviors. A score of “3” on
the speech item was deﬁned as follows: “talking
faster or more to say at times.” Given the goal of
treatment is to increase frequency of speech, an item
score of “3” for this domain would likely lead to false
positives of behavioral disinhibition. Therefore, an
item score of “4” (ie, “Talking more or faster to the
point where he/she is difﬁcult to interrupt”) or above
was regarded as more appropriate as an indicator of
behavioral
disinhibition.
In
addition,
2
items
regarding appearance and sexual interest were
removed, as they were deemed inappropriate for the
anticipated age of participants.
Ratings of side effect severity were provided
by
parents
twice
per
week
and
by
clinicians
biweekly using the same CGI approach discussed
previously.19,24 Ratings ranged from 1 (positive
changes greatly outweigh negative changes. Medi-
cation effects are overall extremely positive) to
6 (negative changes signiﬁcantly outweigh positive
changes. Medication effects are overall extremely
negative).

Treatment Acceptability and Adherence
The 11-item acceptability scale on the Treatment
Evaluation Questionnaire, Parent Version (TEQ-P)33

was used to examine treatment acceptability at the
end of the study. The TEQ-P was developed from the
Treatment Evaluation Inventory (TEI),34 which was
able to delineate between treatments that individu-
als considered acceptable and not acceptable. Each
statement is rated on a Likert scale with a rating
of 1 indicating “strongly disagree” and a rating of 6
indicating “strongly agree.” A score of 55 or higher
indicates high treatment acceptability.33 Carlson
et al19 and Eke20 found high treatment acceptability
using the TEQ-P in studies examining the use of
sertraline for the treatment of selective mutism, with
average scores of 58.6 and 56.8, respectively. The
use of this measure creates continuity with these
previous studies.
Parents reported medication adherence on a form
requiring them to document the time they provided
the medication each day (eg, “Monday at 8:00 am”).
These data were compiled in the form of the per-
centage of days children were provided their medi-
cation within 6 hours of the recommended time.
Data Analysis

Because SSRIs have a delayed onset of therapeutic
effects, an acquisition period of 4 weeks from the
start of the introductory dose was included in the
data analysis for each child. Effect sizes for each

participant were calculated for DBR ratings of
social anxiety, responsive speech, and spontaneous
speech using Cohen’s d, with the following formula:
M (intervention)−M (baseline and placebo phase)/
pooled SD. Pooled SDs were used in the individual
effect size calculations because some participants
had SDs of 0 in the baseline phase due to a complete
absence of target behaviors. Group effect sizes were
also calculated for the DBR ratings of speech and
social
anxiety
with
the
following
formula:
M
(intervention)–M (baseline and placebo)/baseline
SD. Outcomes on supplementary measures of social
anxiety and speech are presented descriptively.
RESULTS

Five girls and 1 boy between 5 and 14 years of age
were enrolled after meeting the inclusion and
exclusion criteria and completed the treatment. The
demographic characteristics of these participants
are presented in Table 2. Previous practitioners for
all participants reported resistance to psychosocial
treatment. Only child 4 continued to receive psy-
chosocial therapy on a weekly basis. According to
his therapist, he was not experiencing improvement
with cognitive behavioral therapy alone; therefore,
he was permitted to enroll in the study while con-
tinuing to receive treatment.
Despite meeting all screening requirements, one
child was
consistently interacting socially with
unfamiliar adults and speaking in social interactions
before the onset of ﬂuoxetine treatment according to

parent ratings. Therefore, a stable baseline trend
indicating selective mutism symptomology could not
be established. In addition, the reliability of this
parent’s ratings were questionable, given that she
reported high ratings of symptomology during the
clinical
interview
but
little
symptomology
on
standardized ratings and observations and 5 weeks
of ratings were not completed. Because there was
failure to demonstrate a trend of symptomatic
behavior
during
the
baseline/placebo
phase,
this child was removed from the analysis per
methodology guidelines provided by Watson and
Workman35 and Christ36 (Fig. 1). For the remainder
of this paper, this participant will be identiﬁed as
“Child R” for “child removed.” Of note, children 1, 2,
4, and 5 also engaged in some of the target behaviors
(ie, social engagement, responsive speech, sponta-
neous speech) during the baseline/placebo phase;
however, these behaviors were sporadic and quickly
returned to a baseline level of evident symptomology
or were far below the level of behaviors expected for
a typical child without selective mutism (eg, child 2:
social engagement).
Social Anxiety

Visual analysis indicated that all children increased
the amount of social engagement with unfamiliar

adults as based on observations by parents recorded
using the DBR system (Fig. 2). Individual effect
sizes on parent completed DBRs of social anxiety
ranged from medium to large (range: 0.50 to 1.51),
with a moderate total group effect of d = 0.79.
Results from the supplementary measures of social
anxiety were mixed, with only 3 of the 5 children
demonstrating improvements on the MASC-2 via
visual analysis. However, reductions in symptom
severity and improvements in functioning were
noted in all other parent and psychiatrist ratings of
shyness and anxiety (Table 3).
Frequency of Speech

For DBRs for responsive speech, individual effect
sizes ranged from medium to large (range: 0.71 to
1.34). The group effect size was also large at
d = 0.90. The effect sizes for DBRs of spontaneous
speech ranged from small to large (range: 0.23 to
0.81) with a large group effect size of d = 0.97.
Results from the SMQ, as well as parent and psy-
chiatrist CGI ratings of selective mutism severity
and improvement, indicated a reduction in symp-
tomology during the ﬂuoxetine treatment phase
(Table 3). Despite these improvements in speech, all
children continued to meet DSM-5 criteria for
selective mutism at the end of the study.

Adverse Events and Behavioral Disinhibition

The initiation of active ﬂuoxetine medication corre-
sponded with incidents of behavioral disinhibition in
2 participants as rated by parents on the P-YMRS.
Child 2 exhibited some mild disruptive behavior (eg,
increased demandingness) during weeks 6 and 7, and
increased restlessness during week 10 of the treat-
ment schedule. Child 3 also exhibited mild disruptive
behavior during weeks 13 and 14. Despite this ﬁnd-
ing, visual inspection indicated improvements in
selective mutism symptomology did not correspond

solely
to
the
onset
of
behavioral
disinhibition
(Figs. 2, 3). Of note, all occurrences of behavioral
disinhibition resolved promptly by the next rating
(ie, in approximately 3 to 4 d). According to the psy-
chiatrist-completed SEFCA, none of the participants
experienced adverse events, aside from the parent-
reported incidents of behavioral disinhibition, during
the ﬂuoxetine treatment. As a group, parents per-
ceived more positive than negative changes during
the ﬂuoxetine phase (M = 2.12, SD = 0.83). Further,
the study psychiatrist also perceived a positive beneﬁt
to risk ratio (M = 2.43, SD = 1.09).
FIGURE 2. Parent DBRs of social engagement with unfamiliar adults by week.

Baseline = no medication (week 1) and placebo; Acquisition = introductory dose (10 mg/d for 2 wk) and therapeutic dose
(20 mg/d for 2 wk); Treatment = therapeutic dose (20 mg/d); Baseline/Intervention administered nonconcurrently across
participants. DBR indicates Direct Behavior Rating.

Treatment Acceptability

Parents found the use of ﬂuoxetine highly accept-
able. A score of 55 or higher on the TEQ-P indicates
an intervention is highly acceptable,33 with the
average across raters in this study being 60.6. Of the
5 parents, 4 indicated they “strongly agreed” ﬂuox-
etine was an acceptable intervention for the problem
behavior, with the parent of child 4 indicating
that she “agreed” with this statement. All parents

“agreed” ﬂuoxetine was effective at addressing the
problem behavior.
Medication Adherence

The study psychiatrist instructed parents to either
give the medication in the morning (n = 3) or in the
evening (n = 2) based on each child’s individual needs.
Parents were found to be adherent 82% to 100% of the

time during the baseline phase. The parents of child 2
who indicated 82% adherence noted they had for-

encouraged to be consistent with providing the medi-

adherent 98% to 100% of the time.
Parent and Teacher Reports of Improvements

In addition to the assessment data regarding
behavioral change for individual children, parents

and teachers provided comments on forms and
feedback during medication management meetings
indicating progress after the onset of ﬂuoxetine
treatment (Table 4).
DISCUSSION

The use of a randomized, single-case, multiple-
baseline design to examine the effects of ﬂuoxetine
on
symptoms
of
social
anxiety
and
speaking
behaviors in this study supports ﬁndings from pre-
vious work that did not use methodological designs
FIGURE 3. Parent DBRs of spontaneous and responsive speech with unfamiliar adults by week.

Baseline = no medication (week 1) and placebo; Acquisition = introductory dose (10 mg/d for 2 wk) and therapeutic dose
(20 mg/d for 2 wk); Treatment = therapeutic dose (20 mg/d). Baseline/Intervention administered nonconcurrently across
participants. DBR indicates Direct Behavior Rating.

10
January 2018
Journal of Psychiatric Practice Vol. 24, No. 1
FLUOXETINE TREATMENT FOR SELECTIVE MUTISM
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

intended for small samples, despite a small number
of participants.11,12 In addition, this study adds
conﬁdence that the improvements in social anxiety
and speaking behaviors in prior studies, such as an
open label study12 and numerous case studies were
not solely due to placebo effects. This was achieved
as children and adolescents in this study served as
their own control (ie, baseline/placebo phase) and
treatment schedules were randomly assigned to
each participant.
Parent-completed
DBRs
of
social
engagement
indicated that participants displayed reductions in
symptoms of social anxiety after the onset of treat-
ment. Despite these reported gains, visual analysis
of graphs indicated that 2 of the children did not
improve on the parent-completed MASC-2. It may be
that DBRs were more sensitive to changes in social
anxiety than the MASC-2. Likewise, it is possible
that children experienced additional anxiety because
they put themselves in social situations with unfa-
miliar adults that they would have avoided in the
past. Nonetheless, the notable improvements on
DBRs of social anxiety and parent-completed CGIs
and psychiatrist-completed CGIs, as well as sponta-
neous parent reports of improvement support the
notion that a meaningful reduction in social anxiety
symptoms was achieved. The treatment of social
anxiety symptoms in children with selective mutism
remains important, given the high comorbidity rate

between social anxiety and selective mutism and the
reconceptualization of selective mutism as an anx-
iety disorder in the DSM-5.1,3

In addition to improvements in social anxiety
symptoms, ﬂuoxetine treatment resulted in a note-
worthy increase in both responsive and sponta-
neous speech. The improvement in both types of
speech is promising, given that the goal of treat-
ment for children with selective mutism is to
increase speech intended for conversation17 (ie,
spontaneous speech). Interestingly, parents per-
ceived
greater
improvement
in
symptoms
and
reduction in severity on CGI ratings of anxiety,
shyness, and mutism than the study psychiatrist.
Of note, all of the participants in this study had
exhibited symptoms for a substantial period of time
(ie, ∼1 to 9 y). All of these children had also failed to
respond to previous psychosocial treatments. Given
the duration and severity of symptoms, parents
likely perceived slight improvements as a sub-
stantial and dramatic change. In contrast, the psy-
chiatrist, who observed more subtle improvements,
was likely more objective in estimating the change
that was occurring. However, this does not mean
that the slight improvements noted by the psy-
chiatrist are unimportant, as the modest changes
achieved from this brief psychopharmacological
intervention are promising when placed in the
context of symptom duration and severity.

Journal of Psychiatric Practice Vol. 24, No. 1
January 2018
11
FLUOXETINE TREATMENT FOR SELECTIVE MUTISM
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

Despite the observed reductions in social anxiety
and improvements in speaking behaviors, all of the
children continued to meet the diagnostic criteria
for selective mutism at the conclusion of the study.
Most
evidence-based
treatments
for
selective
mutism are grounded in the behavioral model of
exposure
and
systematic
desensitization.7,17,37

These approaches often require children to develop
a fear hierarchy, which has frequently been com-
pared with a ladder. Treatment requires that chil-
dren start at the bottom rung of the ladder, which
involves speaking in a slightly anxiety provoking
setting (eg, while playing a game with a trusted
peer) and climb up the ladder to the most fear
provoking task (eg, speaking to a group of unfami-
liar adults). However, attempting to complete the
task at the bottom rung of the ladder may be too
difﬁcult to achieve when anxiety is too severe.
Given the reductions in symptomology found in this
study, ﬂuoxetine treatment may allow children who
previously failed to respond to psychosocial treat-
ment an opportunity to more easily participate in
psychosocial treatments (ie, access the ﬁrst rung of
the
fear
hierarchy
ladder)
leading
to
greater
reductions in social anxiety and improvements in
speaking behaviors than either treatment alone.
The superiority of combined treatments for anxiety
disorders, including social anxiety disorder, has
been
documented
in
the
literature.38
Future
research should examine if the combination of psy-
chosocial treatments with ﬂuoxetine as well as the
manipulation of other treatment variables (ie, dose,
length of treatment) leads to more robust outcomes
in children with selective mutism.
Previous research has found rapid improvements
in speaking behaviors after the initiation of treat-
ment with an SSRI. Some researchers have sug-
gested that the unintended side effect of behavioral
disinhibition
may
be
playing
a
role
in
the
improvement in speech, instead of the intended
anxiety reduction mechanism.9 Two participants in
our study did experience symptoms indicative of
behavioral disinhibition for brief periods of time, as
noted by parents, although reductions in sympto-
mology did not correspond solely with incidents of
behavioral disinhibition; therefore, there was no
apparent functional link between these observed
behaviors. Similar to ﬁndings in previous studies,9

a rapid reduction in symptomology was noted after
the initiation of the medication. Future research

should examine why some children with selective
mutism seem to demonstrate a rapid response to
SSRI treatment.
Aside from reports of the behavioral disinhibition
experienced by 2 participants, the reports from
parents of children in this study revealed no other
adverse events during treatment. These results
suggest that ﬂuoxetine is a safe treatment for
selective mutism in children 5 to 14 years of age,
especially at the doses (ie, 10 and 20 mg/d) used in
this study. Overall, these ﬁndings are consistent
with previous research that has examined the use of
ﬂuoxetine in the treatment of selective mutism and
other internalizing disorders. For example, Black
and Uhde11 examined the use of ﬂuoxetine for the
treatment of selective mutism in children between 6
and 11 years of age and found no statistical differ-
ence in adverse events between the placebo group
and the treatment group. In a study examining
ﬂuoxetine for internalizing disorders at a dosage of
20 mg/d, Birmaher et al39 found a higher prevalence
of adverse events, with approximately half of the
7- to 17-year-old children treated with ﬂuoxetine
experiencing gastrointestinal side effects. Given all
of the side effect data collected in previous clinical
trials indicating a small but reliable number of side
effects with ﬂuoxetine,40 it is likely there would
have been a greater percentage of mild side effects
with a larger pool of participants.
Overall acceptability ratings were higher than in
previous studies examining the use of sertraline for
selective mutism symptoms.19,20 In this study,
parents provided an overall acceptability rating of
60.6, whereas parents in the study by Carlson
et al19 provided an average rating of 58.6, and
parents in the study by Eke20 gave an average
rating of 56.8. It is not surprising that parents
found the intervention acceptable given the noted
improvements, lack of side effects, and the fact that
all of these children had previously failed to respond
to a psychosocial intervention.
Limitations

The sole reliance on parent observations and rat-
ings for the primary outcome measures is a limi-
tation of this study. Future studies should work to
identify an evaluation approach that includes rat-
ings in other settings, such as school. In addition,

FLUOXETINE TREATMENT FOR SELECTIVE MUTISM
Copyright © 2018 Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.

the removal of Child R from data analysis is a
limitation and is one of the weaknesses of a non-
concurrent multiple-baseline design with a priori
randomization to treatment schedules, as addi-
tional time cannot be provided to wait for baseline
ratings to stabilize.35,36 The consistent engagement
in the target behaviors according to parent report
was unexpected, given the intensive screening
process. Although it was not ideal to remove this
child from the analysis, this child did not appear to
be different on any important variable from the
remainder of the sample.
CONCLUSIONS

Through the use of a methodologically sound design
intended to draw conclusions from small samples,
the results of this study add to the evidence that
ﬂuoxetine treatment leads to a reduction in social
anxiety symptoms and an increase in speaking
behaviors in children and adolescents with selective
mutism who have previously failed to respond to
psychosocial interventions. Behavioral disinhibition
does
not
seem
to
have
been
responsible
for
improvements in speaking behaviors. In addition,
participants experienced minimal adverse events
related to the ﬂuoxetine treatment, and parents
found the use of ﬂuoxetine for their child’s symp-
toms highly acceptable. Clinicians treating selective
mutism in children and adolescents who have not
improved with psychosocial treatment may ﬁnd
that ﬂuoxetine treatment leads to reductions in
social anxiety, an increase in responsive and spon-
taneous speech, minimal side effects, and high lev-
els of parent acceptability.
